text: RESPONDENI OF NEW SOUTH WALES | H Gis ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT OF ee NEW SOUTH WALES. —~_— Practice—Time for setting down appeal for hearing—Delay— Appeal Rules, see, Il Sypney, 1. 12, of 22nd August, 1904—Costs. : Nov. 28. a ; ; Where an appellant has a substantial ground of appeal, and has shown Geimen 0.3, bond fides by promptly giving security and taking all other necessary st 'O'Connor JJ. the prosecution of his appeal, the mere failure to set the appeal down 2 CLR.) OF AUSTRALIA. 75 hearing at the proper sittings of the Court, as prescribed by the Appeal Rules, H.C, or A.v* is not in itself sufficient ground for dismissing the appeal for want of 1904 prosecution. ad An appellant filed and served notice of appeal on 7th September, 1904, and BRICKWooD on the next day lodged security, £50, though, under the Appeal Rules, he need Youne anp not have done so till three months later. By r. 12 of sec. III. of the Amended OTHERS.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: Appeal Rules of 22nd August, 1904, the appellant was bound, under these = cireumstances, to set down the appeal for hearing at the sittings of the High Court in November, 1904, whereas, if he had waited until the expiration of the three months allowed for the giving of security, he would not have been obliged to set the appeal down until the subsequent sittings. 'The appeal not having been set down within the time prescribed, certain of the respondents moved to have it dismissed for want of prosecution. 'The motion was dismissed, upon the appellant undertaking to sev the appeal down for hearing at the then present sittings, but the appellant was ordered to pay the costs of the motion. The Court refused to make the payment of these costs by the appellant a condition precedent to the entertaining of the appeal. Moron to dismiss appeal for want of prosecution.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: Notice of appeal was filed and served on 7th September, and on the next day £50 security was lodged by the appellant, but the appeal was not set down for the November sittings, which were the first sittings after the two months allowed by r. 12, sec. IIL, of the Appeal Rules of 22nd August, 1904. It appeared that the appellant's solicitor was under the impression that it was necessary to have the transcript prepared before setting the appeal down for hearing, and its preparation had been unavoidably delayed by the making of necessary copies of documents, and by certain Other causes for which the respondents' solicitors were partly responsible.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: Rich, for the applicants (respondents other than the Minister for Public Works). The appellant, by his delay, has disentitled himself to have his appeal heard. The security was lodged on 8th September, and therefore the appeal should have been set . down for hearing at the present sittings: r. 12, sec.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: IIL of Appeal Rules, 22nd August, 1904, which amended the Amended Rules of 12th October, 1903. There was no reasonable excuse for the delay. It was not necessary to have the transcript complete before setting down.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: Harvey, for the appellant. The Court will not insist upon rigid compliance with the rules when the appellant has a sul stantial ground of appeal, and the respondents have not be prejudiced. The appellant here has a substantial claim again the respondents, and has shown his bond fides by lodging £50 security on the earliest possible day, and by g going to the exp of preparing the transcript for the appeal. He was entitled wait for three months before giving security, and, if he had don so, he need not have set the appeal down until after these sitting The respondents, therefore, are in no worse position than would have been if the appellant had taken all the time to whic he was entitled for each step.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: The difficulty has been caused b his own expedition in giving security. The appellant is prepare to set down the appeal for the present sittings, if the responden will waive the notice. Rich in reply.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: The judgment of the Court was delivered by t Grirrirx C.J. This Court is not inclined to give effect t merely technical points in any case, and in the present case th earlier than by the rules he was bound to do. He need not hay given security until next month, December, and he could then ha \ set the appeal down for hearing at the next sittings of the Court held two months after that time, but, owing to his own expeditio in giving security at the earliest possible moment instead of th latest, it became necessary, technically, to set it down for present sittings, although the appellant seems not to have tho it necessary to do so.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: The appeal appears to have been bro bond fide, and security to have been given at the earliest po (1) 7 Ch, D., 241. by 2 CLR.) OF AUSTRALIA. 'moment, and, if the appellant had done what he was strictly bound to do, he would have set the appeal down on 7th November. It would then have come into the list for these sittings after all the cases now on the paper, so that the respondents really lose nothing in substance, if we allow the appeal to be set down now. Mr.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: Harvey offered to set it down for these sittings, and there is no objection to that on the part of the respondents. Under these circumstances, following the practice of the Court of Chancery, and also, indeed, of the Courts of Common Law, which, in similar cases, were accustomed to allow the appellant to have time, requiring him to give an undertaking to "speed the cause," we think that the proper order to make in this case is that, the appellant undertaking to set the appeal down for these sittings, the application be dismissed, the appellant to pay the costs of the motion. If we were very strict, and insisted on the rigid observance of the rules in these matters, it would probably result in a want of uniformity in the practice, as the expedition required in Sydney and Melbourne would be very much greater than in the capitals of the other States in which sittings are held.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: In some of these the Court sits only twice, and in some only once, in the year. Rich asked to have the payment of the costs made a condition precedent to the entertaining of the appeal. GeirritH C.J.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36

text: It is not usual to make such an order, and there is no special reason for making it in this case. Applicution dismissed wpon the appellant undertaking to set the appeal down for the present sittings. Appellant to pay the costs of the application.
citation: Brickwood v Young and Others and the Minister for Public Works (NSW) [1904] HCA 36
